beats.headline: Records of millions, taken on an unauthorized system.
beats.body: Background-investigation files on about 21 million people were stolen. Auditors had warned for years that key systems were running without a current authorization to operate.
say: Every plan, every signature, every assumption — I will undermine it.
Not tonight. And the deepest gaps aren't in the walls — they're in the decisions above them.
The costliest kind. Welcome to the High Council.

beats.title: THE INNER KEEP
say: Strike the plans, and every wall is built on sand.
Then it's time you met the High Council, where the realm is governed.

beats.title: THE HIGH COUNCIL
beats.subtitle: Risk · Planning · Program · Assessment
say: Layer four is governance, the decisions above any single wall.
Four families rule the keep: Risk Assessment, Planning, Program Management, and Assessment & Authorization.

beats.title: THE HIGH COUNCIL
say: RA, PL, PM, and CA. The seer, the cartographer, the steward, and the inspector.

beats.protects: Reads the omens: what could go wrong, and how badly.
beats.reality: Identifying and analyzing risk, including vulnerability scanning and threat assessment.
say: The Seer, Risk Assessment — though this seer never merely guesses.
It doesn't read omens; it measures them: what could go wrong, how likely, and how badly.
In reality, that's analyzing risk with hard evidence — vulnerability scans and threat intelligence.

beats.title: Risk Assessment
beats.plain: Assess the risk to the system — threats, vulnerabilities, likelihood, and impact.
beats.why: Risk is the compass; it tells you which controls actually matter for your system.
say: RA-3 is the formal risk assessment, the basis for every choice.

beats.title: Vulnerability Monitoring and Scanning
beats.plain: Scan for vulnerabilities, and act on what you find.
beats.why: An unscanned, unpatched service is the unseen crack in the wall.
say: How do we find the cracks before the Null does?
RA-5, vulnerability monitoring and scanning, hunts them down.

beats.quote: Monitor and scan for vulnerabilities in the system and hosted applications [organization-defined frequency and/or randomly in accordance with organization-defined process] and when new vulnerabilities potentially affecting the system are identified and reported;
beats.cite: NIST SP 800-53r5  ·  RA-5 Vulnerability Monitoring and Scanning
say: Monitor and scan for vulnerabilities in the system and hosted applications [organization-defined frequency and/or randomly in accordance with organization-defined process] and when new vulnerabilities potentially affecting the system are identified and reported;

beats.title: Risk Assessment
bullets: RA-3 Risk Assessment — assess threats, vulnerabilities, and impact
RA-5 Vulnerability Monitoring and Scanning — scan for vulnerabilities and remediate
RA-7 Risk Response — respond to the risks you find
beats.mnemonic: “Know the risk before you spend a coin.”
say: Risk first. It tells you where to spend your strength.

beats.oath: I do not guess the storm; I measure it.
say: The Seer's oath. I do not guess the storm; I measure it.

beats.protects: Draws the map of the defense before the march.
beats.reality: System security and privacy plans, rules of behavior, and security architecture.
say: It draws the map of the defense before the march.
In reality, that's the system security and privacy plan, the rules of behavior, and the security architecture.

beats.title: System Security and Privacy Plans
beats.plain: Write a security and privacy plan for the system — the single source of truth.
beats.why: The plan says what the system is, who runs it, and which controls protect it.
say: PL-2 is the system security and privacy plan, the master map.

beats.title: Security and Privacy Architectures
beats.plain: Design a security and privacy architecture, on purpose, up front.
beats.why: Architecture decides where the walls go before a single stone is laid.
say: And PL-8 plans the architecture, defense by design.

beats.quote: Develop security and privacy plans for the system that:
beats.cite: NIST SP 800-53r5  ·  PL-2 System Security and Privacy Plans
say: Develop security and privacy plans for the system that:

beats.title: Planning
bullets: PL-2 System Security and Privacy Plans — the system security & privacy plan
PL-4 Rules of Behavior — rules of behavior for users
PL-8 Security and Privacy Architectures — security & privacy architecture
say: No plan, no defense, just luck.
The Cartographer's oath. No march without a map.

beats.protects: Governs the whole realm's defense, above any single wall.
beats.reality: Organization-wide governance: the security and privacy program, roles, risk strategy, and resources.
say: It governs the whole realm's defense, above any single system.
In reality, that's organization-wide governance: the security and privacy program, roles, risk strategy, and resources.

beats.title: Risk Management Strategy
beats.plain: Set an organization-wide strategy to manage risk.
beats.why: PM steers the whole program; these controls run org-wide, not per-system.
say: Who sets the strategy for everything?
PM-9, the risk management strategy for the entire organization.

beats.title: A note on Program Management
bullets: PM controls are organization-wide, not tied to one system
They are deployed independently of the Low / Moderate / High baselines
Think governance: leadership, strategy, resources, and oversight
say: Important: PM controls aren't selected per system from a baseline.
They run across the whole organization, all the time.

beats.quote: Develops a comprehensive strategy to manage:
beats.cite: NIST SP 800-53r5  ·  PM-9 Risk Management Strategy
say: Develops a comprehensive strategy to manage:

beats.title: Program Management
bullets: PM-2 Information Security Program Leadership Role — name the security program leadership
PM-9 Risk Management Strategy — the organization-wide risk strategy
PM-11 Mission and Business Process Definition — define mission and business processes
PM-31 Continuous Monitoring Strategy — the continuous monitoring strategy
beats.mnemonic: “Govern the whole, not just the parts.”
say: Program management is the realm thinking as one.
The High Steward's oath. One realm, one strategy — always.

beats.protects: Tests the defenses and signs off before the gates open.
beats.reality: Assessing controls, formally authorizing systems to operate, and continuously monitoring them.
say: The Inspector, Assessment, Authorization, and Monitoring.
It tests the defenses, and signs off before the gates open.
In reality, that's assessing controls, formally authorizing a system to operate, and watching it continuously.

beats.title: Authorization
beats.plain: An authorizing official formally accepts the risk and authorizes the system to operate.
beats.why: Authorization is a named, accountable leader putting their signature on the risk.
say: CA-6, authorization, the authorizing official says 'I accept this risk.' The agency we opened with ran key systems without this signature.
No. But it puts a name on the wall the day something does. And CA-7 keeps watching after the ink dries.

beats.expand: ATO = Authorization to Operate, granted by the Authorizing Official (AO)
beats.plain: A senior leader's formal, signed decision that a system's remaining risk is acceptable — so it may go live. It accepts risk; it does not guarantee safety.
say: So a real person signs for the risk?
The authorizing official — a senior, accountable leader — grants the A-T-O, and owns that decision.

beats.title: Control Assessments
beats.plain: Independently assess whether the controls actually work.
beats.why: Selecting a control isn't enough; CA-2 checks it truly does its job.
say: CA-2 plans and runs the assessment, using procedures from companion doc 800-53A.

beats.plain: A living to-do list of every known weakness you haven't fixed yet — each with an owner and a due date. It's how honest teams drive risk down over time.
say: And the gaps we find but can't close today?
They go on the POA&M. CA-5 requires it, and the authorizing official keeps an eye on it.

beats.quote: Select the appropriate assessor or assessment team for the type of assessment to be conducted;
say: Select the appropriate assessor or assessment team for the type of assessment to be conducted;

beats.title: Assessment, Authorization, and Monitoring
bullets: CA-2 Control Assessments — assess that controls work (per 800-53A)
CA-5 Plan of Action and Milestones — track weaknesses in a POA&M
CA-6 Authorization — authorize the system to operate
CA-7 Continuous Monitoring — monitor continuously
say: Assess, authorize, and never stop monitoring.

beats.oath: Test before you trust; watch after you sign.
say: The Inspector's oath. Test before you trust; watch after you sign.

beats.title: The High Council drives the RMF
bullets: RA → categorize and assess risk
PL → plan; PM → govern the whole program
CA → assess, authorize, and monitor the system
say: If a layer maps onto the framework, it's this one.
Risk, planning, governance, and assessment, the work behind the RMF lifecycle.

beats.q: A new system is ready to go live. Who formally accepts the risk to operate it?
say: Scenario: go-live day. Whose signature is required? Pause, and answer.
beats.why: CA-6, Authorization, is where a senior official formally accepts the risk to operate.

beats.q: Where are known weaknesses tracked until they're fixed?
say: And where do we record the gaps we haven't closed yet?
beats.why: Known weaknesses are tracked in a Plan of Action and Milestones — CA-5 — until they're fixed.

beats.title: Episode 04 — the High Council
lines: RA finds and ranks risk with evidence (RA-3, RA-5, RA-7).
PL plans the defense; PM governs the whole organization (PL-2, PL-8; PM-9, PM-2).
CA assesses, authorizes (the ATO), tracks gaps (POA&M), and monitors (CA-2, CA-5, CA-6, CA-7).
beats.mnemonic: Govern the whole; test it, sign it, watch it.
say: Notebook: this layer is the brain — it decides what the walls are even for.
The realm is governed. Now, to where it's all built.
Built? I'll poison it at the source.
Then we descend to the Forge.
